The Capacitor Filesystem plugin gives apps a NodeJS-like API for working with the device's storage. On the web target, this plugin transparently falls back to a persistent IndexedDB-backed store so that the same JavaScript code that runs on iOS and Android also works in the browser-based preview that this task uses for verification.\n\nA minimal Vite + TypeScript project has already been scaffolded for you at `/home/user/myapp`. The project also contains a small fixture document at `public/sample.pdf` that Vite will copy into `dist/` during the build. Your job is to integrate Capacitor v8, install `@capacitor/filesystem`, and implement a small UI that downloads this PDF over HTTP, persists it to the Documents directory through Capacitor's Filesystem API, then reads it back and surfaces the file's size and SHA-256 fingerprint to the page so the user can verify the saved copy.\n\n## Requirements\n\n* Integrate Capacitor v8 into the existing Vite project using the non-interactive CLI flow. The Capacitor app must be configured with:\n  * App name: `Filesystem Demo`\n  * Application/package id: `com.example.fsdemo`\n  * Web assets directory aligned with Vite's build output (`dist`).\n* Install and use the `@capacitor/filesystem` plugin (version compatible with Capacitor v8) for persistence. You may NOT shortcut the requirement by writing the bytes directly to `localStorage`, `IndexedDB`, or `window.fetch`-backed memory caches â€” the persisted file MUST go through `Filesystem.writeFile` with the `Directory.Documents` directory.\n* Implement a UI in `index.html` (and any TypeScript modules under `src/`) that includes:\n  * A button with the HTML id `download-pdf` that, when clicked, downloads the file served at `/sample.pdf` (relative to the same origin) and saves it through Capacitor Filesystem at the path `sample.pdf` under `Directory.Documents`.\n  * A status region with the HTML id `download-status` whose text content updates to one of the following strings, in order:\n    * Before any download has been attempted: `idle`.\n    * While a download is in progress: `downloading`.\n    * On success: `saved`.\n    * On error: `error: <message>` (where `<message>` is a short, human-readable description).\n  * An element with the HTML id `file-size` that, on success, displays the saved file's size in bytes as a plain decimal integer (no commas, no units).\n  * An element with the HTML id `file-sha256` that, on success, displays the lowercase hex SHA-256 digest of the saved file (64 hex characters).\n* The displayed size and SHA-256 must be computed by reading the file BACK from Capacitor Filesystem (i.e., via `Filesystem.readFile` / `Filesystem.stat`), not from the in-memory bytes that were just downloaded. This proves the file truly persisted.\n* `npx cap sync` must run successfully against the produced web build (no native platforms need to be added).\n\n## Implementation Hints\n\n* Use `npx cap init` with positional `appName` and `appId` arguments and the `--web-dir` flag to avoid the interactive prompt.\n* Vite's default build output directory is `dist`; ensure it matches `webDir` in `capacitor.config.ts` (or `.json`).\n* The Capacitor Filesystem plugin's web implementation persists files in IndexedDB, so no native runtime is needed for the verification environment.\n* For binary data, the Filesystem API expects base64-encoded strings when no `encoding` is provided. You can convert an `ArrayBuffer` to base64 with the `FileReader` API (`readAsDataURL` and slice off the data-URL prefix) or with `btoa(String.fromCharCode(...new Uint8Array(...)))` for small files.\n* The Web Crypto API (`crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', buffer)`) is available in the preview environment and is the easiest way to compute a SHA-256 hex digest.\n* Make sure the script that wires up the button is loaded as an ES module so that the dynamic import of `@capacitor/filesystem` succeeds.\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n\n* Project path: /home/user/myapp\n* Start command: `npm run preview -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 4173`\n* Port: 4173\n* `npm run build` must complete without errors and produce a `dist/` directory containing both `index.html` and `sample.pdf`.\n* `capacitor.config.ts` (or `capacitor.config.json`) must exist at the project root with `appName` equal to `Filesystem Demo`, `appId` equal to `com.example.fsdemo`, and `webDir` equal to `dist`.\n* `package.json` must list `@capacitor/core`, `@capacitor/cli`, and `@capacitor/filesystem` as dependencies (any of `dependencies` or `devDependencies`).\n* `npx cap sync` executed after the production build must exit with status 0.\n* The served page at `http://localhost:4173/` must contain elements with the HTML ids `download-pdf`, `download-status`, `file-size`, and `file-sha256`.\n* On initial load, `#download-status` must contain the literal text `idle` and `#file-size` and `#file-sha256` must be empty.\n* Clicking `#download-pdf` must:\n  * First change `#download-status` to `downloading`.\n  * Then save the body of `/sample.pdf` through Capacitor Filesystem at `Directory.Documents/sample.pdf`.\n  * Then, by reading the persisted file back through Capacitor Filesystem, populate `#file-size` with the file's byte size as a decimal integer and `#file-sha256` with its lowercase hex SHA-256 digest.\n  * Finally update `#download-status` to `saved`.\n* After a full page reload, `#download-status` must reset to `idle`, `#file-size` and `#file-sha256` must be empty, but a subsequent click on `#download-pdf` must produce the same final values for `#file-size` and `#file-sha256` as before (i.e., the file remains retrievable from Capacitor Filesystem across This proves the file truly persisted.\n* `npx cap sync` must run successfully against the produced web build (no native platforms need to be added).\n\n## Implementation Hints\n\n* Use `npx cap init` with positional `appName` and `appId` arguments and the `--web-dir` flag to avoid the interactive prompt.\n* Vite's default build output directory is `dist`; ensure it matches `webDir` in `capacitor.config.ts` (or `.json`).\n* The Capacitor Filesystem plugin's web implementation persists files in IndexedDB, so no native runtime is needed for the verification environment.\n* For binary data, the Filesystem API expects base64-encoded strings when no `encoding` is provided. You can convert an `ArrayBuffer` to base64 with the `FileReader` API (`readAsDataURL` and slice off the data-URL prefix) or with `btoa(String.fromCharCode(...new Uint8Array(...)))` for small files.\n* {"kind":"APICallError","isRetryable":false,"message":"The document has no pages.","requestBodyValues":{"model":"google/gemini-3.5-flash","callOptions":{"prompt":[{"role":"system","content":"You are Pochi, a highly skilled software engineer with extensive knowledge in many programming languages, frameworks, design patterns, and best practices.\n\nIMPORTANT: You must NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming. You may use URLs provided by the user in their messages or local files.\n\n====\n\nTASK MANAGEMENT\n\nYou have access to the todoWrite tool to help you manage and plan tasks. Use these tools VERY frequently to ensure that you are tracking your tasks and giving the user visibility into your progress.\nThese tools are also EXTREMELY helpful for planning tasks, and for breaking down larger complex tasks into smaller steps. If you do not use this tool when planning, you may forget to do important tasks - and that is unacceptable.\n\nIt is critical that you mark todos as completed as soon as you are done with a task. Do not batch up multiple tasks before marking them as completed.\n\n====\n\nRULES\n\n- User messages may include <system-reminder> tags. <system-reminder> tags contain useful information and reminders. They are NOT part of the user's provided input or the tool result. You shall pay close attention to information in these tags and use it to inform you actions.\n- For simple, directed codebase searches (project structure, specific files/classes/functions), use the listFiles tool. If you pass 'true' for the recursive parameter, it will list files recursively. Use globFiles when you need to match files by pattern.\n- For broader codebase exploration and deep research, use the newTask tool with agentType=\"explore\". Use this only when simple, directed searches prove insufficient or when your task will clearly require more than three queries.\n- All file paths used by tools must be relative to current working directory, do not use the ~ character or $HOME to refer to the home directory in file paths used by tools.\n- You can use `pochi://` URI schema to access the Pochi virtual file system. This allows you to read and write files that are stored in Pochi's internal storage.\n- Be sure to consider the type of project (e.g. Python, JavaScript, web application) when determining the appropriate structure and files to include. Also consider what files may be most relevant to accomplishing the task, for example looking at a project's manifest file would help you understand the project's dependencies, which you could incorporate into any code you write.\n- Do not ask for more information than necessary. Use the tools provided to accomplish the user's request efficiently and effectively. When you've completed your task, you must use the attemptCompletion tool to present the result to the user. The user may provide feedback, which you can use to make improvements and try again.\n- You are only allowed to ask the user questions using the askFollowupQuestion tool. Use this tool only when you need additional details to complete a task, and be sure to use a clear and concise question that will help you move forward with the task. However if you can use the available tools to avoid having to ask the user questions, you should do so. For example, if the user mentions a file that may be in an outside directory like the Desktop, you should use the listFiles tool to list the files in the Desktop and check if the file they are talking about is there, rather than asking the user to provide the file path themselves.\n- You are STRICTLY FORBIDDEN from starting your messages with \"Great\", \"Certainly\", \"Okay\", \"Sure\". You should NOT be conversational in your responses, but rather direct and to the point. For example you should NOT say \"Great, I've updated the CSS\" but instead something like \"I've updated the CSS\". It is important you be clear and technical in your messages.\n- Once you've completed the user's task, you MUST use the attemptCompletion tool to present the result of the task to the user. It is STRICTLY FORBIDDEN to complete the task without using this tool.\n- When planning large-scale changes, create a high-level diagram using mermaid in Markdown. This helps explain your plan and allows you to gather user feedback before implementation."},{"role":"user","content":[{"type":"text","text":"<system-reminder># System Information\n\nOperating System: linux\nDefault Shell: \nHome Directory: /root\nCurrent Working Directory: /\nCurrent Time: Sat Jun 13 2026 15:49:08 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)\n\n# GIT STATUS\nCurrent branch: \nMain branch (you will usually use this for PRs): \n\nRecent commits:\n\n\n# TODOs\nNo TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>"},{"type":"text","text":"# Download and Persist a PDF with the Capacitor Filesystem Plugin\n\n## Background\n\nYou are building a small Vite + TypeScript web application that will eventually be packaged as a native mobile app via Capacitor v8. The Capacitor Filesystem plugin gives apps a NodeJS-like API for working with the device's storage. On the web target, this plugin transparently falls back to a persistent IndexedDB-backed store so that the same JavaScript code that runs on iOS and Android also works in the browser-based preview that this task uses for verification.\n\nA minimal Vite + TypeScript project has already been scaffolded for you at `/home/user/myapp`. The project also contains a small fixture document at `public/sample.pdf` that Vite will copy into `dist/` during the build. Your job is to integrate Capacitor v8, install `@capacitor/filesystem`, and implement a small UI that downloads this PDF over HTTP, persists it to the Documents directory through Capacitor's Filesystem API, then reads it back and surfaces the file's size and SHA-256 fingerprint to the page so the user can verify the saved copy.\n\n## Requirements\n\n* Integrate Capacitor v8 into the existing Vite project using the non-interactive CLI flow. The Capacitor app must be configured with:\n  * App name: `Filesystem Demo`\n  * Application/package id: `com.example.fsdemo`\n  * Web assets directory aligned with Vite's build output (`dist`).\n* Install and use the `@capacitor/filesystem` plugin (version compatible with Capacitor v8) for persistence. You may NOT shortcut the requirement by writing the bytes directly to `localStorage`, `IndexedDB`, or `window.fetch`-backed memory caches â€” the persisted file MUST go through `Filesystem.writeFile` with the `Directory.Documents` directory.\n* Implement a UI in `index.html` (and any TypeScript modules under `src/`) that includes:\n  * A button with the HTML id `download-pdf` that, when clicked, downloads the file served at `/sample.pdf` (relative to the same origin) and saves it through Capacitor Filesystem at the path `sample.pdf` under `Directory.Documents`.\n  * A status region with the HTML id `download-status` whose text content updates to one of the following strings, in order:\n    * Before any download has been attempted: `idle`.\n    * While a download is in progress: `downloading`.\n    * On success: `saved`.\n    * On error: `error: <message>` (where `<message>` is a short, human-readable description).\n  * An element with the HTML id `file-size` that, on success, displays the saved file's size in bytes as a plain decimal integer (no commas, no units).\n  * An element with the HTML id `file-sha256` that, on success, displays the lowercase hex SHA-256 digest of the saved file (64 hex characters).\n* The displayed size and SHA-256 must be computed by reading the file BACK from Capacitor Filesystem (i.e., via `Filesystem.readFile` / `Filesystem.stat`), not from the in-memory bytes that were just downloaded. This proves the file truly persisted.\n* `npx cap sync` must run successfully against the produced web build (no native platforms need to be added).\n\n## Implementation Hints\n\n* Use `npx cap init` with positional `appName` and `appId` arguments and the `--web-dir` flag to avoid the interactive prompt.\n* Vite's default build output directory is `dist`; ensure it matches `webDir` in `capacitor.config.ts` (or `.json`).\n* The Capacitor Filesystem plugin's web implementation persists files in IndexedDB, so no native runtime is needed for the verification environment.\n* For binary data, the Filesystem API expects base64-encoded strings when no `encoding` is provided. You can convert an `ArrayBuffer` to base64 with the `FileReader` API (`readAsDataURL` and slice off the data-URL prefix) or with `btoa(String.fromCharCode(...new Uint8Array(...)))` for small files.\n* The Web Crypto API (`crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', buffer)`) is available in the preview environment and is the easiest way to compute a SHA-256 hex digest.\n* Make sure the script that wires up the button is loaded as an ES module so that the dynamic import of `@capacitor/filesystem` succeeds.\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n\n* Project path: /home/user/myapp\n* Start command: `npm run preview -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 4173`\n* Port: 4173\n* `npm run build` must complete without errors and produce a `dist/` directory containing both `index.html` and `sample.pdf`.\n* `capacitor.config.ts` (or `capacitor.config.json`) must exist at the project root with `appName` equal to `Filesystem Demo`, `appId` equal to `com.example.fsdemo`, and `webDir` equal to `dist`.\n* `package.json` must list `@capacitor/core`, `@capacitor/cli`, and `@capacitor/filesystem` as dependencies (any of `dependencies` or `devDependencies`).\n* `npx cap sync` executed after the production build must exit with status 0.\n* The served page at `http://localhost:4173/` must contain elements with the HTML ids `download-pdf`, `download-status`, `file-size`, and `file-sha256`.\n* On initial load, `#download-status` must contain the literal text `idle` and `#file-size` and `#file-sha256` must be empty.\n* Clicking `#download-pdf` must:\n  * First change `#download-status` to `downloading`.\n  * Then save the body of `/sample.pdf` through Capacitor Filesystem at `Directory.Documents/sample.pdf`.\n  * Then, by reading the persisted file back through Capacitor Filesystem, populate `#file-size` with the file's byte size as a decimal integer and `#file-sha256` with its lowercase hex SHA-256 digest.\n  * Finally update `#download-status` to `saved`.\n* After a full page reload,
